segments.title: A long-range flying boat from Blackburn's oversized-aircraft experience
segments.summary: Blackburn drew on its earlier large-aircraft work when it began the R.B.1 Iris, a very large long-range reconnaissance flying boat designed to Air Ministry Specification R.14/24.
segments.body: The Iris is introduced as evidence that Blackburn could produce an elegant aircraft as well as its more familiar carrier types. The company drew on experience with oversized aircraft structures gained from the Great Kookaburra air biplane. Blackburn began work in 1924 on a very large flying boat intended for long-range reconnaissance. Major J. D. Rennie designed it to Air Ministry Specification R.14/24. The combination of structural scale and an official reconnaissance requirement placed the Iris in a different operational category from a short-range deck aircraft.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the Iris, Blackburn's previous large-aircraft experience, its 1924 start, Rennie, and Specification R.14/24.

segments.title: Three engines for a safer overwater patrol
segments.summary: The Iris used three engines to reduce the danger of a forced descent at sea, retaining altitude at full load on two engines and remaining airborne after losing two.
segments.body: For a long-range flying boat, engine redundancy was a practical maritime safety feature rather than just a performance statistic. The Iris carried three engines specifically to reduce the risk of a forced descent at sea. Dr. Clarke explains that it could maintain height at full load on any two engines, leaving near-normal flight available after one failure. Even after losing a second engine, the aircraft could keep going, though at a less desirable altitude. That reserve mattered because an engine failure far from shore did not have to become an immediate landing and survival emergency.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the three-engine arrangement, its forced-descent rationale, and the aircraft's performance after one or two engine failures.

segments.title: Hull form and the difficult transition to flight
segments.summary: The Iris combined a plywood-covered wooden hull with a sharp two-step V-bottom to run cleanly on the water and shorten the costly, demanding takeoff transition.
segments.body: The Iris used a plywood-covered wooden hull with a streamlined, sharp two-step V-bottom. Dr. Clarke describes this form as exceptionally clean-running on the water, which helped the aircraft accelerate toward flying speed. During that transition the engines had to operate at full power, consuming fuel while placing time, energy, and strain on the airframe. He distinguishes the relatively settled conditions of floating and airborne flight from the more difficult takeoff and landing phases. Reducing the time spent between those states therefore improved economy as well as limiting stress on the aircraft. He closes by emphasizing the Iris's rarity: only four were built.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the Iris hull, explains why fast water acceleration matters for takeoff, and notes that only four aircraft were built.
